name: Brussels Griffon
description: The Brussels Griffon is a charismatic Belgian toy breed with an almost human, frowning expression that became popular with Belgian coachmen as a ratter before being refined into a companion dog favoured by the Belgian royal family. These small but self-possessed dogs are highly intelligent, sensitive, and form intense bonds with a single person, often becoming the quintessential 'velcro dog.' Their brachycephalic (flat-faced) structure requires careful attention to breathing and exercise in hot weather.

conditions_detail.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions_detail.description: BOAS is a complex of anatomical abnormalities in flat-faced breeds including stenotic nares, elongated soft palate, hypoplastic trachea, and everted laryngeal saccules. These combine to severely restrict airflow and cause chronic respiratory distress.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Surgical correction of stenotic nares, elongated soft palate, and everted saccules significantly improves quality of life. Weight control and avoiding heat/exercise are essential management measures.
prevention_tips: Select breeding stock with wider nostrils and better airway scores
Use BOAS functional grading schemes before breeding
Avoid breeding from severely affected dogs

conditions_detail.name: Patellar Luxation
conditions_detail.description: Patellar luxation occurs when the kneecap slips out of its normal groove in the femur. It can be medial (inward) or lateral (outward) and ranges from grade 1 (occasional slipping) to grade 4 (permanent dislocation).
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Grade 1-2 cases are managed conservatively with weight control and physiotherapy. Grade 3-4 typically require surgical correction to deepen the patellar groove and realign the tibial crest.
prevention_tips: Breed from dogs with normal patellar scores
Avoid high-impact activities in affected puppies
